content:
12 min read
Just now

--

Two solo builds — a local-business operating system and a family AI assistant — the single conclusion they forced on me and the exact pipeline that got me there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ten weeks ago, Project Nimbus was a CRM tool. Today it’s an operating system for local business — a platform where a chemist shop, a bakery, a general store and a logistics business each get software that speaks their language, all on shared infrastructure.
I didn’t change that scope in a planning meeting. I changed it in a decision log, on a Sunday, alone, with an AI pair sitting across the table.
At the same time, in a different corner of my life, I was building Project Null — a private AI assistant for my family, running locally on a Mac Mini, with no cloud and no budget. Different domain, different stack, different users. Same way of working. And, it turned out, the same lesson.
That’s really what this article is about. Two products with nothing in common except how they were built, both pointing at the same future for our craft. I’ll make the argument and then show you exactly how it worked in practice because a claim without the evidence behind it isn’t worth much.
The old shape of the job
For two decades, product management used to be a coordination job. The PM sat in the middle of a triangle – engineering, design, and business – translating between them. Your value was in the handoffs: the spec engineering could build from, the roadmap leadership could fund, and the ticket QA could test.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The dirty secret was that most of the job was latency management. You wrote a requirement on Monday; you saw it built on Friday three weeks later, and you discovered it was wrong on the demo after that. Product management was the discipline of being careful, because being wrong was expensive and slow to discover.
Both of my projects broke that assumption. When I decided Nimbus should be multi-vertical instead of a single pharmacy app, the architectural consequences were enormous — every business type needed different fields to track, a different order flow to follow, and a starting setup that matches how that trade actually works. In the old world, that’s a quarter of the work and a team. In the build, the decision and its implementation happened the same afternoon, because I wasn’t writing the code; I was directing it.
The bottleneck moved. It’s no longer important how quickly you can construct. It is all about your ability to think clearly. When delivery is nearly free, being right is the entire game.
That’s the theory. Here’s what it looked like in practice.
What it actually looks like
The best way to make the argument concrete is to trace one requirement through the full pipeline — from a sentence in CLAUDE.md to a merged pull request with a passing test suite. I’ve written that up in full detail separately, but here’s how the steps actually flow.
Step 1 — CLAUDE.md: the constitution. Everything starts here, before a line of product code exists. Product vision, business modules, architecture rules, dev workflow — all in one document, structured in the order a product actually thinks. The table of contents is the product’s order of operations.
Step 2 — Tool stack: locked and wired. The stack gets defined next – exact tools, exact versions – with one rule: use these; do not deviate. Then the tools get connected to each other, so a commit message moves a work item, and a merged PR triggers a deploy. Technology is no longer a topic of discussion; it is taken for granted.
Step 3 — MRS: the market case. The market requirements document captures the broad need – what problem, for whom, and why now? This is the layer that justifies everything downstream. If it’s fuzzy here, it’s wrong everywhere else.
Step 4 — PRS: requirements with no ambiguity. The PRS narrows the market case into product requirements precise enough to test. Not “users can manage orders” but a literal state machine with blocked transitions and explicit rules about what constitutes verified.
Step 5 — Features: Every feature gets split by one question- is this difference in data or in behaviour? Different fields or statuses go into a config file. Different behaviour gets its own module. It sounds simple because it is and that simplicity is what stops a product serving multiple business types from turning into a mess.
Step 6 — User stories: vertical slices. Features decompose into user stories small enough to build and verify in a single sitting. Each is a vertical slice — UI, data, and behaviour together. A story you can demo is a story you can finish.
Step 7 — Build: a fixed sequence, every time. Each story gets its own branch, named for its AB# work item. The build sequence never changes — schema first, types from the schema, hooks, components, and guard logic tested beside the code that uses them. The AI builds against the sequence; the PM holds the architecture.
Step 8 — QA: 'Done' is a result, not an opinion. A story isn’t done when the code compiles. It’s done when every linked test has passed across four tiers — unit, integration, functional, and E2E; the changelog has a new line; and the work item has flipped to Resolved.
Step 9 — Bug loop: Defects enter the system immediately. A bug can surface at any point — during testing or in production. Either way it becomes a tracked defect, a child of the story it broke, fixed on its own branch through the same sequence. Found during testing, it tells you the story wasn’t ready to close. Found in production, it tells you something the QA tier missed. Either way, you know exactly where to look.
Step 10 — Release and close the loop. The release is scripted, tagged, and boring — exactly what a release should be. Wrong decisions get superseded in the decision log, the original intact, and the revision below. The next sprint starts from everything the last one knew, including the things that didn’t work.
The pipeline is familiar. The person running it is not.
Here is what’s actually different. None of the above steps are unusual. Product teams have been doing versions of them for years. What’s unusual is who executes them.
At the start of a session, I give Claude Code a single prompt. Something like: “implement AB#63 — Rx pending badge” or “plan the next sprint based on open stories in priority order.” That’s it. What happens next is the part that still surprises me.
Claude Code opens the Azure DevOps board through MCP, reads the work item, pulls the acceptance criteria, checks which stories are dependencies, and determines what’s next in the sequence. It branches off dev with the correct name — feature/AB#63-rx-pending-badge — then opens CLAUDE.md and reads the build rules before writing a single line of code. It follows the sequence: schema first, types from the schema, hook, component, and guard logic with tests beside it. When the code is done, it writes the test cases, runs the suite locally, and opens a pull request with the AB# in the title. The work item transitions to In Review automatically on merge.
For sprint planning the prompt is different, but the autonomy is the same. “Plan the next sprint — pick from open AB# stories and prioritise by dependency order and business vertical.” Claude Code reads the full backlog, checks done ratios per feature, identifies blockers, and returns a ranked sprint plan with story points and a rationale for each pick. I review it. I decide. But the analysis that used to take a planning meeting happens in under two minutes.
It doesn’t do any of this because it’s clever. It does this because every rule was written down precisely enough to follow. The CLAUDE.md isn’t documentation. It’s the operating manual for an autonomous build agent.
The PM’s job in that pipeline isn’t any of the ten steps. It’s the document that makes all ten steps possible.
What one person and an AI actually shipped in 33 days
Numbers are easy to manufacture and easy to dismiss. So before the argument, the raw record: everything below comes directly from the Git history, Azure Boards, and the test suite of Project Nimbus — generated by Claude Code against the actual repository.
The project closed 70 tracked work items, merged 36 pull requests, and shipped three production releases. Not in one go at the end. One every ten days, each one planned, tagged, and deployed. By the end of the month, two full business verticals – Pharmacy and Courier – were live in production. A third, General Store, was baselined and ready to go.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

152 commits across 33 weekend days — Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays — from 1 May to 13 July 2026. Three production releases, each ten days apart.Across 11 feature modules, the build tracked 87 user stories — 68 of them closed, the remaining open scope pointing exactly at where the roadmap still sits. Orders and customers are nearly fully closed. Courier and Catalogue carry the most open stories, which is honest: those are the modules where the next sprint begins. The done-ratio per feature tells you more about where a product actually is than any headline number.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

87 user stories across 11 feature modules. High done-ratio means closed scope. Low done-ratio shows exactly where the roadmap still sits.The total feature code stands at 8,639 lines across 147 files, with a further 6,000 lines of platform infrastructure underneath — shared libraries, app state stores, database migrations, edge functions, seed data, and end-to-end tests. The platform layer isn’t glamorous work. It’s what makes the feature layer possible at that speed.
The discipline numbers are where I would push back on anyone who assumes speed and rigour are a tradeoff. The test suite finished the period at 273 test cases across 53 files — 30 Vitest unit tests and 23 Playwright end-to-end suites — representing roughly 22% of application code by line count. Every commit followed the conventional commit format without exception: 50 feature commits, 33 fixes, 19 chores, and 10 docs. For every fix, the commit ratio was held at 1.5:1 across the full period, meaning most work was forward motion, not rework.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

70 work items, 36 PRs, 3 releases — and the discipline numbers that made it possible. Speed and rigour are not a tradeoff.That last point is worth pausing on. A 1.5:1 commit ratio sustained over 33 days, with a test suite at 22% of code, is not what fast-moving solo builds typically look like. It’s what they look like when the architecture is holding and the decisions upstream were made carefully.
There is one more number worth noting separately: 40 logged architectural and product decisions, each tracked as a work item in Azure Boards with a DEC prefix, each with a reason and a date. Most solo MVPs have zero decision traceability. When a decision changed, it wasn’t deleted — it was superseded, the original intact, and the revision below it. The document got smarter rather than just longer. Those 40 records are not a delivery metric. They’re the reason the other metrics are possible.
Two verticals in a month wasn’t about working faster. It was about a decision made early that made every vertical after the first one cheaper to build. Each new business type needed a configuration file, not a new codebase. One good call at the start multiplied across eleven feature modules. That’s what the numbers are actually showing — the right structural decision doesn’t just save time once. It keeps paying out.
A note on where this is heading institutionally
This isn’t only a solo-builder’s observation. In a March 19, 2026 post on Anthropic’s Claude blog, Catherine Wu, Head of Product for Claude Code, described the same shift from the inside — and her account lines up closely with what I found building Nimbus and Null in my spare time.
The traditional PM playbook, she wrote, as
